channel.title: Part 0: Introduction on Chege's Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Part 0: Introduction on Chege's Blog

item.title: Introduction
item.description:
Investor Key Fund/Vehicle Period Annualized Returns After Fees
Jim Simons Medallion Fund 1988 – 2018 (30) 39.1%
George Soros Quantum Fund 1969 – 2000 (31) 32.0%
Steven Cohen SAC 1992 – 2003 (11) 30.0%
Peter Lynch Magellan Fund 1977 – 1990 (13) 29.0%
Warren Buffett Berkshire Hathaway 1965 – 2018 (53) 20.5%
Ray Dalio Pure Alpha 1991 – 2018 (27) 12%

But time in the market is king - at least that’s what Malkiel taught me. Ranking the returns using Bayesian methods gives: Simmons 28.0%, Soros 26.9%, Cohen 26.2%, Lynch 26.2%, Buffet 24.6%, Dalio 24.0%. Maybe I did something wrong - Dalio shouldn’t be close to Buffet. And I thought Buffet would be higher up in the new ranking.

item.title: Prologue
item.description:
Fall 1990. Simons had spent 12 years searching for a successful investing formula. In 1989, his net returns were -4.0%. In 1988, he had netted 9.0%. In 1990, he netted 55.0% (77.8% before fees).

With Elywn Berlekamp, game theorist professor at UCB, Simons built a model for selecting idea trades based on data.

Berlekamp knew their approach was alchemic - no cash flows, interest rate forecasts, product research. Some of the trade recommendations didn’t make sense.
